Marc Andreessen: Will a16z Go Public & Why Labour Displacement with AI is Wrong?

100 min read · video (interview) · 2026-03-30
Marc Andreessena16zventure capitalAIlabor displacementlump of labor fallacySilicon ValleyEuropestartupsfirm buildingconsumer surplusSchumpeterian economics
Source: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=c4tvVKDhpiY

Summary

Marc Andreessen sits down with Harry Stebbings (20VC) for a wide-ranging interview covering venture philosophy, a16z's structure, AI and labor economics, Silicon Valley's dominance, Europe's challenges, and personal reflections on 30 years of tech. The interview reveals Andreessen's operating philosophy at a depth rarely seen in shorter formats — from why he thinks introspection is overrated in venture (the "scalded stove" problem of learning too well from mistakes), to his conviction that AI labor displacement fears are "100% incorrect" and rooted in the Marxist lump of labor fallacy.

On venture, Andreessen makes several sharp claims: the mistake of omission always outweighs the mistake of commission; "don't do diamonds in the rough, only do diamonds" (consensus-breaking picks rarely work unless you're Peter Thiel); overfunding is as dangerous as underfunding but no founder ever listens; and the core of the business is always early stage — "baking the cake" in the first two years. On a16z going public: "there's nothing we're missing today that we could solve by going public." On Europe: he wishes it would flourish but every head of state he's met knows the Draghi Report answers and just can't execute them.

The AI section is the most quotable. Andreessen argues current tech layoffs are 100% explained by COVID overhiring and interest rate normalization, not AI — companies are "at least overstaffed by 25%, most by 50%, many by 75%," and AI is just the "silver bullet excuse." His economic framework: ~99% of AI's value will accrue to users as consumer surplus (Schumpeterian economics), not to the companies building it. AI is "the most hyperdemocratic technology we've ever seen" — the best AI is the app you download on your phone. Coders using AI work MORE hours, not fewer, because productivity unlocks demand. The lump of labor fallacy fails because demand is not fixed, human wants are infinite, and technology creates new job categories at a 100% historical base rate across 250 years.

Key Takeaways

  • Introspection is overrated in venture — learning from mistakes creates the "scalded stove" problem, making you pass on the next Google/AI/crypto because the previous one burned you
  • Mistake of omission > mistake of commission — in venture, the $10M loss hurts but passing on Google's $100B upside is catastrophic
  • "Don't do diamonds in the rough, only do diamonds" — the consensus pick is usually right; contrarian picks rarely work unless you're Peter Thiel
  • Overfunding is as dangerous as underfunding — "nobody ever does the lock box" and high valuations set hurdle rates that kill you in down rounds
  • The first two years are everything — "baking the cake" — if you leave the sugar out, you can't pour it on later
  • a16z won't go public — "there's nothing we're missing today that we could solve by going public"
  • Silicon Valley is MORE centralized than ever — COVID remote work reversed hard; ~100% of quality AI companies are in a 20-mile radius of Palo Alto
  • AI layoffs are COVID overhiring, not AI — "AI until December wasn't good enough to do any of the jobs they're cutting"
  • Companies are overstaffed 25-75% — AI is the "silver bullet excuse" for cuts that needed to happen anyway
  • 99% of AI value → consumer surplus — Schumpeterian economics: users capture almost all value, companies building AI get ~1%
  • AI is hyperdemocratic — the best AI is the $20/month app on your phone; 5 billion people will have it soon
  • Labor displacement is "100% incorrect" — coders with AI work MORE hours because productivity unlocks demand
  • Europe knows what to do but can't do it — every head of state knows the answers from the Draghi Report, they just "don't like the trade-offs"
  • Gulf states (UAE, Saudi, Qatar) impressed him most — among heads of state he's met in last 5 years
  • Biggest regret: passing on Anduril Series A — "the politics and cultural elements scared us off" — now all-in on defense tech
  • Most memorable founder meeting: Zuckerberg at 19 — Sean Parker talked the entire time, Zuck didn't say a word, turned out he was on a "vertical learning curve"
  • Copywriting process: "2 years of frustration, 2 hours of writing" — arguing with himself internally until it explodes onto the page

Full Transcript

I'm competing with myself. Life just gets a lot simpler if you just assume everything is your own fault. There's nothing that we're missing today that we could solve by going public. >> The man who has built one of the greatest firms of our time. They manage over $90 billion. And I think it's AI >> and have invested in some of the most generational companies. >> This entire labor displacement thing is 100% incorrect. It's completely wrong. Essentially, every large company is overstaffed. It's at least overstaffed by 25%. I think most large companies are overstaffed by 50%. I think a lot of them are overstaffed by 75%. >> This was a very special one for me and I hope you enjoy the episode. Ready to go. Mark, you probably don't know this, but I started this show when I was 18 years old, and you were one of three names that I wanted to have on the show back in 2015. Uh but the problem is learning from mistakes, sometimes it's good and sometimes it's bad, right? Um and if you just talk business for a moment, like in the venture mindset, this is a very big problem. There's a founder version of the mistake, there's a venture version of the mistake. The founder version of the mistake is if a founder starts a company in a category and the founder doesn't work, the founder is then emotionally angry at that category for the rest of his life. um and will not acknowledge uh when there's something that's going to work in that category. Um and I I've just seen that like over and over and over again. Um and and that's fine because most founders go on to do other things and that's fine and good and it it generally doesn't damage them from a business standpoint. In venture, the same thing the same thing happens. If you invest in a category or if you invest in a kind of company uh or you invest in a kind of founder um and it doesn't go well, it's extremely easy to learn from the mistake, right? And to basically say, "All right, I touched that hot stove. I'm never doing it again." And then you know you can tell me what happens next, right? Which is the next thing shows up in pattern matches and it's the thing that you should invest in and you have the chance to invest in and but but you touch the scald scalded stove and you know you're you know you're learning from your mistakes, right? You're doing the responsible thing and so you don't do it. And so I think there's something that's particularly pernicious about learning from your mistakes in venture capital. And then I think that's also somewhat true about life. You get married multiple times as they say it's the triumph of hope over experience. Like I think probably you want hope to triumph over experience. um in in that domain and and I think there's a lot of other domains of life in which that's probably true. I totally understand what you're saying when especially when you say about hey it's easy to lose money in a sector and then think the sector by nature is cursed or it's so difficult you can't make money in healthcare you can't make money in X I >> I'm old enough to remember internet search like you can't make money in internet search like the internet search companies in the 1990s did not work out well >> so then how do you when you are guiding conversation when you are guiding partners how do you ensure that they have a fresh mind with every new company and every new investment and are not plagued by the downsides have bluntly lost money before. >> Yeah. So, by by the way, just the other example, by the way, is AI. AI was a tremendously good way to lose a lot of money in venture capital from 19 from 1945 to 2017. So, there I mean, look, when I was getting my computer science degree in the late ' 80s, like AI was like AI was like the one field that you knew would never succeed. Like, there had actually been an investment boom for AI in the 80s and it failed. And everybody, including all the computer scientists, were like, "Yeah, this field is dead." and that and that happened like five times over the course of of AI over the last 80 years. Um and so like you know again another great example. So so look I I I think a couple things in terms of how we we run our firm or how you run a firm like this. So so one is as you well know that there are but very important there are two categories of mistakes right there's there's the mistake of commission and there's the mistake of omission or there's the mistake of cost and there's the mistake of opportunity cost. Um and so of course the mistake of cost is you invest $10 million in a startup it fails you loses the money you lose the money that's bad. um the the the the the mistake of omission is you don't invest in Google and you lose hundred billion dollars of opportunity cost right um and so of course venture is like the most polar you know it's the most polarized possible economic field um in which in which this is true and by the way if you're running a if you're running a you know if you're running a bond business or something you know debt debt business or something you know where you can't you you just can't lose money or the whole thing doesn't work then obviously you can't run in this kind of model in that case you better learn from your mistakes but but in venture you right in venture I think you're always much more worried about the mistake of omission than you're worried about about the mistake of commission. And then to your question, like I think in a lot of ways that's the key thing that Ben and I do at this point in our lives um and in in our roles at the firm is you know we're not like micromanaging the the investment decisions at the firm and we have I think spectacular you know senior partners and junior partners that are doing a great job of that and we're we're in the room for it and so forth but like we're not we're generally not advocating for against a deal but what we are trying to do is to get everybody to constantly have this let's say risk forward um worry about the mistake of omission before o over over the mistake of commission uh mindset. This anti- scalded stove phenomenon, you know, we we just routinely remind people like, yeah, you're emotional about this because of your bad experience 3 years ago or 6 years ago or 10 years ago and just like let that go. You know, you you no longer have to pay for that sin and and and and you're completely liberated to be able to kind of let that emotion uh you know, fade into the distance and and be able to focus on the opportunity in front of you. Um, and he and he actually he actually wrote a paper on on on this topic. And I'll just give you his conclusion, which is also my conclusion. Arthur Rock, for people who don't know, he invested in Apple and Intel, right, in the seed rounds and like in many many other great companies for like 30 years was that uh he would have been a better venture investor had he fed all of the business plans uh and pitch deck straight into the shredder uh upon receiving them. Um and if he had spent 100% of his time on the resume um and I think that's basically right, which is the great the great founders will, you know, basically buy you enormous upside that may break rules in all kinds of directions um and may break precedent in all kinds of directions. um and the world's best business plan executed by a mediocre team will almost certainly get lapped by a great team.
